old_claim.note: Relativity (Special & General) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Accurate GPS timing requires corrections for both special-relativistic motion and general-relativistic gravitational effects on satellite clocks.

source_shape.source_locator: GPS paragraphs: quantifies the opposing special- and general-relativistic clock effects and says engineers had to correct them for GPS to provide accurate time.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: NIST calculates the net satellite-clock offset from special and general relativity and states that operational GPS corrects for it.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The accumulated timing shift would translate into rapidly growing range error, so the scoped accurate positioning and time service depends on applying both theories.

ontology_before: Relativity was modeled as an optional accuracy enhancement to GPS.
ontology_after: Special- and general-relativistic clock corrections are required for the accurate GPS timing service.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-gps-navstar-logistics-boundary: relativity_special_general remains required for gps.
would_reject_if: The GPS node is explicitly narrowed to a short-duration laboratory demonstration whose accuracy does not require satellite-clock correction.
short_reason: NIST states that GPS engineers had to correct both relativistic effects to deliver accurate time.
